extra.reasoning:
The variable `rash` appears in exactly one rule: `interaction_drop` (priority 100, the highest), which sets acuity := self_care (lowest level) when severe_headache AND stiff_neck AND vision_loss AND dizziness AND confusion AND hemoptysis AND leg_swelling AND rash are all true. Because rash only ever serves as a conjunct enabling a rule that LOWERS acuity, the monotonicity property is violated.

Counterexample (witness is the rash=true assignment): severe_headache=T, stiff_neck=T, vision_loss=T, dizziness=T, confusion=T, hemoptysis=T, leg_swelling=T, chest_pain=F.

With rash=FALSE (all else identical): interaction_drop does not fire. Firing rules include raise_stiff_neck/raise_hemoptysis/raise_dizziness (priority 3 -> emergent) plus several priority-2 urgent rules. Highest priority is 3, so acuity = emergent (the maximum level).

With rash=TRUE: all eight conjuncts of interaction_drop are satisfied, so it fires at priority 100, beating every other rule (priorities 2 and 3), setting acuity = self_care (the minimum level).

Enum order is self_care < routine < urgent < emergent. Setting rash true drops acuity from emergent to self_care — strictly lower — directly violating the property that setting rash true never lowers acuity.
